clinical_indications: Presence of branchial arch anomaly presenting as a sinus
Swelling in the neck (with or without pain)
Large anomalies (sinuses) causing asymmetry of the neck, dyspnea, dysphagia, or dysphonia
Infected sinuses (surgery best undertaken when lesion is quiescent)

preauth.label: Clinical notes with signs, symptoms, indications, planned line of management and advise for admission
USG Neck/ Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC)

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication & advise for procedure?
USG Neck/FNAC confirming the diagnosis and indication for surgery?
Was the clinical picture and imaging indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge available?

common_queries: Clinical picture or imaging not indicative of surgery.
Clinical notes lacking detailed history, signs, symptoms, indication, or advice for procedure.
USG Neck/FNAC not confirming diagnosis or indication for surgery.
Indoor case papers (ICPs) lacking daily vitals or line of treatment.

clinical_key_pointers: Branchial arch anomalies result due to persisting remnants of the branchial cleft and are usually lined by squamous epithelium and contain thick, turbid fluid full of cholesterol crystals.
These anomalies may present as cyst, sinus or fistula in childhood or early adulthood.

Second arch anomalies are the commonest and usually presents as a swelling or sinus in the upper neck at the junction of the upper third and middle third of the sternomastoid muscle at its anterior border.
The inner opening in case of fistulas are located in the tonsillar fossa.
The cysts/sinuses or fistulas may get frequently infected.
First arch fistulas are related to parotid gland and may be related to the facial nerve in their deeper course.
Third and fourth branchial arch fistulas are uncommon and are related to the thyroid gland in their course.
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Treatment is best undertaken when the lesion is quiescent.
